items.title: tfcoach
items.text:
A lightweight Terraform linter that enforces team conventions.

  • Fast CLI built with Cobra
  • Simple rule engine
  • CI-friendly output and exit codes

In this documentation:

  • Get started with tfcoach
  • Rules index
  • How to contribute

items.title: Ideas
items.text: This page contains ideas about up-coming rules.

items.title: Core
items.text:
• Required Provider in terraform block
  • naming
  • plural for lists
  • no resource-type in name
  • name_convention
  • output-format {name}{type}
  • file names
  • variables in variables.tf
  • outputs in output.tf
  • etc
  • placement
  • tags
  • for_each / count
  • remote_backend?
  • terraform_data instead of null
  • variables always needs description

items.title: License
items.text: tfcoach is licensed under the Apache License 2.0. See the LICENSE file for details.

items.title: Supported Versions
items.text: Only the latest release of tfcoach is supported with security updates. Users are encouraged to always upgrade to the most recent version.

items.title: Reporting a Vulnerability
items.text:
If you discover a security vulnerability, please report it via the GitHub Security Advisories page of this repository.

Do not disclose security issues publicly until they have been addressed.

We will review and respond to all reports as quickly as possible.

items.title: Contributing
items.text:
We welcome contributions!

  • Fork the repo and create a feature branch.
  • Add tests for new rules or features.
  • Run make test before submitting a PR.
  • Ensure your commits are signed.

Thank you for improving tfcoach!

items.title: Semantic Commits
items.text:
We are using conventional commits to release this project.

To streamline the whole process, we have enabled squash-commits on merge. So you just need to name your PR correctly.

items.title: Pre-Commit
items.text: We also have defined some pre-commit rules. You can install these hooks via make init-precommit, provided that you have installed the pre-commit tool beforehand.

items.title: Logging
items.text: tfcoach uses Go's standard log/slog package with a globally configured default logger.

items.title: Enabling logs
items.text:
Set the TFCOACH_LOG environment variable before running tfcoach:

TFCOACH_LOG=DEBUG tfcoach lint .
Value Effect DEBUG All messages (debug, info, warn, error) INFO Info, warn, and error messages WARN Warn and error messages only ERROR Error messages only JSON JSON output to stderr, DEBUG level

If TFCOACH_LOG is unset or has an unrecognised value, the level defaults to ERROR.

items.title: Writing log statements
items.text:
Import log/slog directly — no need to pass a logger around:

import "log/slog"

slog.Debug("parsing file", "path", filePath)
slog.Info("rule applied", "rule", rule.ID(), "issues", len(issues))
slog.Warn("config not found, using defaults")
slog.Error("failed to read file", "err", err)

Every log entry automatically includes the timestamp and the source file + line number where the call was made.

items.title: Output
items.text: Logs are written to stderr, keeping stdout clean for lint reports and JSON output.

items.title: Release
items.text: We use Github Action to release using semantic-release and goreleaser.

items.title: Strategy
items.text:
We release as needed, for example if we have added some rules or fixes any bug.

The release is currently manually and gets triggered by admins.

If you need to get a release, simply create an Issue or mention it in your pr.

items.title: ReleaseFlow
items.text:
sequenceDiagram
    autonumber
    participant Dev as Developer
    participant DeployWF as release.yml (orchestrator)
    participant ReleaseEnvironment as Environment
    participant ReleaseGate as release-actions.yml (reusable)
    participant SR as semantic-release
    participant GH as GitHub (Tags/Releases, GHCR)
    participant GR as GoReleaser

    Dev->>DeployWF: manual dispatch Release-Gate
    DeployWF->>ReleaseEnvironment: wait for approval
    Dev->>ReleaseEnvironment: Approve Deployment
    ReleaseEnvironment->>DeployWF: Start deployment
    DeployWF->>Release: uses: ./.github/action/action.yml
    Release->>SR: analyze commits (conventional commits)
    SR-->>ReleaseGate: decision (release? yes/no)
    alt New release
        SR->>GH: generate changelog + create tag + release
        ReleaseGate-->>DeployWF: outputs {released:true, tag:vX.Y.Z, version:X.Y.Z}
        DeployWF->>GH: checkout ref = tag
        DeployWF->>GR: build artifacts (binaries)
        DeployWF->>GH: publish release assets
        DeployWF->>GH: buildx + push Docker images to GHCR
    else No release
        ReleaseGate-->>DeployWF: outputs {released:false}
        DeployWF--x GH: skip build & push
    end

items.title: Artifacts
items.text:
We release multiple artifacts:

Type Arc Link Docker Container arm64 and amd64 for linux https://github.com/Marcel2603/tfcoach/pkgs/container/tfcoach%2Ftfcoach Executable arm64 and amd64 for linux/darwin/windows https://github.com/Marcel2603/tfcoach/releases

items.title: Writing rules
items.text:
Rules implement:

type Rule interface {
  ID() string
  META() RuleMeta
  Apply(file string, f *hcl.File) []Issue
  Finish() []Issue
}

Parse with hclsyntax.ParseConfig, iterate body.Blocks, or use the optional AST walker for nested/expr-heavy checks. Return issues with precise hcl.Range. Keep rules single-purpose and fast.

Depending on what the rule needs to assert, you may report issues for each file independently (in Apply) or collect information and report after all files have been checked (in Finish).

The ID follows this pattern: package.name

items.title: Tools
items.text: Tools are go-scripts that help your local development. They will not be bundled to tfcoach.

items.title: Requirements
items.text:
• Create a folder under tools
  • Exclude it from go build by adding //go:build tfcoach_tools as first line

items.title: Generate Doc Pages
items.text:
This tool will generate the following pages from the code:

  • Rules Overview (uses information from internal api)
  • Usage (uses cobra doc package)
  • generates nav-section inside zensical-config
  • Plain rule-page if it does not exist

items.title: How to execute
items.text: make generate-documentation

items.title: HCL Testing
items.text: This tool helps to understand the hcl-syntax api.

items.title: How to execute
items.text:
go run tools/cmd/hcl-test/main.go examples/compliant/main.tf
  • It will print what hcl will parse, so that you can test with the syntax tree

items.title: Homebrew
items.text:
brew tap Marcel2603/tap
brew install tfcoach

items.title: From release
items.text: Download the latest binary from the Releases page and place it in your $PATH.

items.title: From docker
items.text:
docker pull ghcr.io/marcel2603/tfcoach/tfcoach:latest
docker run -v $PWD:/app ghcr.io/marcel2603/tfcoach/tfcoach:latest lint .

items.title: From source
items.text:
go install github.com/Marcel2603/tfcoach@latest

This will install tfcoach into your $GOPATH/bin or $GOBIN.

items.title: First run
items.text: tfcoach lint .

items.title: tfcoach
items.text:
Tiny Terraform coach

tfcoach [flags]

items.title: Options
items.text: -h, --help   help for tfcoach

items.title: Exit Codes
items.text: Code Meaning 0 OK

items.title: tfcoach lint
items.text:
Lint Terraform files

tfcoach lint [path] [flags]

items.title: Options
items.text:
-c, --config string              Custom config file path (default current directory)
  -f, --format string              Output format. Supported: json|compact|pretty|educational (default "educational")
  -h, --help                       help for lint
      --include-terragrunt-cache   Include Terragrunt cache in scanned files
      --no-color                   Disable color output
      --no-emojis                  Prevent emojis in output

items.title: Exit Codes
items.text: Code Meaning 0 No issues found 1 Issues found 2 Runtime error

items.title: tfcoach print
items.text:
Print tfcoach JSON report in another format

tfcoach print [path] [flags]

items.title: Options
items.text:
-c, --config string   Custom config file path (default current directory)
  -f, --format string   Output format. Supported: json|compact|pretty|educational (default "educational")
  -h, --help            help for print
      --no-color        Disable color output
      --no-emojis       Prevent emojis in output

items.title: Exit Codes
items.text: Code Meaning 0 No issues found 1 Read error 2 Conversion error

items.title: tfcoach version
items.text:
Print the version number

tfcoach version [flags]

items.title: Options
items.text: -h, --help   help for version

items.title: Exit Codes
items.text: Code Meaning 0 OK

items.title: Configuration
items.text:
Multiple aspects of tfcoach can be configured at different levels.

See in this chapter:

  • Where tfcoach searches for configuration
  • What can be configured

items.title: Disable or fine-tune rules
items.text:
Rules can be disabled at will with rules.<rule_id>.enabled: false.

Some rules may allow for further configuration using the rules.<rule_id>.spec map, please refer to the rule-specific documentation for more information.

items.title: Output format
items.text:
Several output formats are supported under output.format:

  • educational (default): issues grouped by rule in a colorful human-friendly format including explanation
  • pretty: issues grouped by file in a colorful human-friendly format
  • compact: one line per issue with location information for quick scanning, sorted by severity
  • json: detailed output optimized for machine-parsing

Color can be disabled with output.color: false.

Emojis can be disabled with output.emojis: false (they get replaced with equivalent text).

Note: The output formats above only affect lint results. Log messages (debug, info, warn, error) are written to stderr and configured separately via the TFCOACH_LOG environment variable. See Logging for details.

Issues found in .terragrunt-cache directories are usually not wanted so disabled by default. These directories can be scanned by setting the property output.include_terragrunt_cache: true.

items.title: Example
items.text:
Example .tfcoach.yml (same options available with the JSON format):

rules: # map to restrict rule configurations
  core.example_rule: # rule_id of the rule you want to configure
    enabled: false  # decide to enable or disable the rule (enabled by default)
    spec: { }  # config specific configuration, see rule documentation
output:
  format: pretty  # see "--help" for supported output formats
  color: true  # enable or disable color; if set to false, equivalent to the "--no-color" flag
  emojis: true  # enable or disable emojis; if set to false, equivalent to the "--no-emojis" flag
  include_terragrunt_cache: false  # enable or disable terragrunt-cache scanning; if set to true, equivalent to the "--include-terragrunt-cache" flag

items.title: Exclude whole files from scanning or reporting
items.text:
Additionally, it is possible to exclude complete paths from reporting issues.

This can be configured via the .tfcoachignore file. It uses the .gitignore convention (see Git docs) for listing files and directories to ignore.

items.title: Where to configure
items.text: You can configure tfcoach via file and partially via env.

items.title: Local configuration
items.text:
To configure via file, you need to create one of the following files in the same directory where you execute tfcoach:

  • .tfcoach.yml
  • .tfcoach.yaml
  • .tfcoach.json
  • .tfcoach (the syntax for .tfcoach is JSON)

It is possible to tell tfcoach to look for configuration in another location with the --config flag:

tfcoach lint --config ~/.my-config-files . # if the argument is a directory, tfcoach will look for the above-mentioned names
tfcoach lint --config ../my-tfcoach-config.yml . # if the argument is a file, it will be used as-is

items.title: Global configuration
items.text:
In order to avoid repeating the same config in multiple directories, you can define a global config in one of the two following directories:

  • $HOME/.config/tfcoach/
  • $HOME/.tfcoach/

where $HOME is the home-directory of your operating system.

items.title: Loading order
items.text:
The configurations are applied in the following order, so that each potentially overrides values from the previous ones:

  • Default config from the repository
  • Global config if it exists
  • Local config (from the current directory or the alternative location provided via --config)
  • Environment variables
  • Command flags (see --help)

items.title: Ignore Rules
items.text:
To ignore rules, you have 2 options:

  • Add a comment on top of the file # tfcoach-ignore-file: core.rule_id1,core.rule_id2
  • Add a comment above the Terraform block to exclude the next block from issuing an error # tfcoach-ignore: core.rule_id1,core.rule_id2

items.title: Core
items.text: Rule Summary Avoid Type in Name Names shouldn't repeat their type. Avoid using hashicorp/null provider With newer Terraform version, use locals and terraform_data as native replacement for hashicorp/null Enforce Parameter Order Enforce parameters should follow a consistent order Enforce Variable Description To understand what that variable does (even if it seems trivial), always add a description File Naming File naming should follow a strict convention. Naming Convention Terraform names should only contain lowercase alphanumeric characters and underscores. Required Provider Must Be Declared All providers used in resources or data sources are declared in the terraform.required_providers block. Use a cloud backend to store the state To store the Terraform state securely, define a cloud backend

items.title: core.avoid_null_provider
items.text: Enforces that the hashicorp/null provider is not used.

items.title: Why
items.text:
The hashicorp/null provider was widely used in older Terraform versions for "glue logic", but it's now considered obsolete and discouraged.

You should not use it anymore and replace it with terraform_data for null_resource and locals for null_data_source.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any usage of null_resource or null_data_source

items.title: Bad
items.text:
resource "null_resource" "config" {
  triggers = {
    config = var.config
  }
}

data "null_data_source" "example" {
  inputs = {
    full_id = "${var.name}-${var.region}"
  }
}

items.title: Good
items.text:
resource "terraform_data" "config" {
  input = var.config
}

locals {
  full_id = "${var.name}-${var.region}"
}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.avoid_type_in_name
items.text: Enforces that the block-type got not repeated in the block-name.

items.title: Why
items.text: Resource names shouldn’t repeat their type; this causes redundancy.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any repatation of the type-definition inside the name

items.title: Bad
items.text:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "s3_bucket_example" {} # repeats s3 and bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "aws_example" {} # repeats aws
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "test_bucket" {} # repeats bucket
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "s3_test" {} # repeats s3

items.title: Good
items.text:
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "ui" {}
resource "aws_s3_bucket" "this" {}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.enforce_parameter_order
items.text:
Enforce the ordering of parameters as recommended by the Terraform docs:

  1. If present, the count or for_each meta-argument
  2. Resource-specific non-block parameters
  3. Resource-specific block parameters
  4. If required, a lifecycle block
  5. If required, the depends_on parameter

This rule applies to the following blocks:

  • resource
  • data
  • module
  • ephemeral
  • output
  • variable

items.title: Why
items.text: Using consistent and predictable ordering of parameters reduces the cognitive complexity and can lead to smaller diffs.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any resource block for which the parameters order differs from the recommendation

items.title: Bad
items.text:
resource "aws_instance" "web1" {
  count = 1
  ami = 4321
  # depends_on should always come last
  depends_on = [
    aws_s3_bucket.s3
  ]
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags]
  }
}

resource "aws_instance" "web2" {
  ami = 1234
  instance_market_options {
    market_type = "spot"
    spot_options {
      max_price = 0.002
    }
  }
  # this non-block parameter should come before the block parameter "instance_market_options"
  availability_zone = "custom-az"
}

items.title: Good
items.text:
resource "aws_instance" "web1" {
  count = 1
  ami   = 4321
  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags]
  }
  depends_on = [
    aws_s3_bucket.s3
  ]
}

resource "aws_instance" "web2" {
  ami               = 1234
  availability_zone = "custom-az"
  instance_market_options {
    market_type = "spot"
    spot_options {
      max_price = 0.002
    }
  }
}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.enforce_variable_description
items.text: Enforces that all declared variables have a non-empty description

items.title: Why
items.text: Even though the intent of variables may seem trivial at first, the variable name itself usually does not carry enough information to ensure it stays that way. A good description can save much debugging time.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any variables with no description or an empty description

items.title: Bad
items.text:
variable "test" {
  type = string
}

variable "test2" {
  type = string
  description = ""
}

items.title: Good
items.text:
variable "test" {
  description = "some descriptive text"
  type = string
}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.file_naming
items.text: Enforces that different type of terraform-resources are written in the correct files

items.title: Why
items.text:
Consistent file-structure across multiple projects. Keeps scalability and error analysis simple.

Example "I want to see resources that I load from external (data-resources). I can just open data.tf"

items.title: Mapping TF-Type to File
items.text: Type Filename output outputs.tf variable variables.tf locals locals.tf provider providers.tf data data.tf

items.title: Mapping of Terraform Block
items.text:
The Terraform Block is separated in different files based on Blocks and Attributes. In general, the "terraform"-Block is allowed to be in following files:

  • backend.tf
  • terraform.tf
Filename Blocks Attributes backend.tf cloud, backend terraform.tf required_provider ,provider_meta required_version,experiments

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: References
items.text: • https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/language/style#file-names

items.title: core.naming_convention
items.text: Enforce the naming convention.

items.title: Why
items.text: Consistent naming improves module reuse and keeps downstream references simple.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any block not following the snake_case naming convention (a-z0-9_)

items.title: Bad
items.text: resource "aws_s3_bucket" "Foo" {}

items.title: Good
items.text: resource "aws_s3_bucket" "foo" {}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.required_provider_must_be_declared
items.text: Enforce that all providers used in resources or data sources are declared in the terraform.required_providers block.

items.title: Why
items.text: Using non explicitly declared providers usually leads to bugs when trying to apply changes.

items.title: Triggers
items.text: • Any resource or data block for which the provider is not declared

items.title: Bad
items.text: resource "aws_s3_bucket" "this" {}

items.title: Good
items.text:
terraform {
  required_providers {
    aws = {
      source  = "hashicorp/aws"
      version = "~> 5.0"
    }
  }
}

resource "aws_s3_bucket" "this" {}

Note that the provider declaration and the resource usage do not need to be in the same file.

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, besides the option to enable or disable the rule

items.title: core.use_cloud_backend
items.text: This rule should enforce that no local statefile is being used.

items.title: Why
items.text: If you store the state locally (default backend), it will be impossible to run terraform from other computers.

items.title: Triggers
items.text:
• No backend or cloud block inside terraform
  • backend-block of type local

items.title: Bad
items.text:
terraform {
 backend "local" {
 }
}

or no backend at all

items.title: Good
items.text:
terraform {
  backend "s3" {
    bucket = "mybucket"
    key    = "path/to/my/key"
    region = "us-east-1"
  }
}

items.title: Configuration
items.text: There is currently no configuration flags for that rule, beside the option to enable or disable the rule
